competencies.competency_text: Observes and identifies the natural (insects, plants, birds, animals, geographical features, sun and moon, stars, planets, natural resources) and social (houses, relationships) components in their immediate environment
competencies.justification: The Life I Hold requires students to identify wildlife (tiger, barking deer, Indian golden gecko, red sandalwood) and ecosystem types (forest, delta, mangrove) sustained by the river. Where I Begin, Where I Flow has students identify the river's geographical features — source in the Western Ghats, tributary network, delta formation, and the Bay of Bengal.
sections: Where I Begin, Where I Flow

competencies.competency_text: Identifies natural and humanmade systems that support their lives (water supply, water cycle, river flow systems, seasons, life cycle of plants and animals, food, household items, transport, communication, electricity in the home)
competencies.justification: C-2.1 explicitly names 'river flow systems' and 'water supply' — the thematic spine of this chapter. The Many Ways I Support Lives (an entire dedicated section) has students identify how Godavari supplies drinking water, irrigation, and industrial water for towns and villages. Dams—Storing My Water, Changing My Flow covers the humanmade dam system. When a River Runs Dry! connects the absence of water supply to cascading human hardship, requiring students to identify the river as a foundational life-support system.
sections: Where I Begin, Where I Flow
The Many Ways I Support Lives
Dams—Storing My Water, Changing My Flow

competencies.competency_text: Connects changes in the environment and the lives of their family and community, as communicated by elders and through local stories (changes in occupation, food habits, resources, celebrations, communication)
competencies.justification: What Pollutes Me and Why it Matters includes a Discuss task (T-11) explicitly asking students to ask parents or elders about the condition of nearby water bodies when they were young, and to compare that to the present. Let us reflect T-22 repeats this elder-consultation pattern for local rivers, and T-20 asks students to research rivers in their own state. The architecture compels intergenerational environmental inquiry — exactly the demand of C-2.3.
sections: What Pollutes Me and Why it Matters

competencies.competency_text: Discusses how to prepare for emergency situations (smoke, fire, small injuries, burns, electrical safety, unseasonal rains, fallen trees) based on discussions with family and community, or personal experiences
competencies.justification: When a River Floods is an entire dedicated section on flood as an emergency situation caused by unseasonal or excess rain — explicitly named in C-3.2. The Flood Safety Tips infographic (Activity 6, p.33) structures before/during/after flood safety actions. Activity 7 (T-17) compels students to role-play as a civic leader planning flood prevention and disaster response, which is discussion-based community preparedness — the precise operation C-3.2 defines.

competencies.competency_text: Demonstrates how natural resources can be shared, maintained, and conserved (trees, use of rainwater, benefits of millets)
competencies.justification: What Pollutes Me and Why it Matters (T-15) asks students to list three conservation actions for water bodies. When a River Runs Dry! presents a conservation ethic (turn off taps, fix leaks, reuse water, National Water Mission, water ATMs) and frames these as shared civic responsibilities. Let us reflect T-27 asks students to design a city that keeps the river clean and ensures water security — demonstrating conservation at a systems level. Together these sections compel students to demonstrate conservation of water as a shared natural resource.
sections: What Pollutes Me and Why it Matters

competencies.competency_text: Performs simple inquiry related to specific questions independently or in groups
competencies.justification: What Pollutes Me and Why it Matters includes Activity 3 (T-13) — a hands-on experiment comparing sugar vs oil in water, observing solubility — and Activity 4 (T-14) — comparing images of a clean river vs an algae-covered river with specific questions to answer. Let us reflect T-26 asks students to independently design a simple water filter using everyday materials. All three are structured inquiry tasks with specific guiding questions, satisfying C-6.1's demand for independent or group inquiry.
sections: What Pollutes Me and Why it Matters
